insight: The recency-anchor gradient is CONFIRMED but WEAK: linear slope = -0.000216 (score per age-day), R² = 0.0103. Older keystones trend toward lower TF-IDF retrieval proxy scores, but the relationship is noisy — R² of 1% means age explains almost none of the variance. The gradient exists as an emergent property but is not a strong predictor.
rationale: 30 Rhetorical Keystones scored against 195 Eblets via TF-IDF proxy queries. Score range 0.0–0.309. Linear fit on age_days→top_tfidf_score yields slope -0.000216 (negative = recency-favoring) but near-zero R². The hypothesis is directionally correct but architecturally insufficient as a forgetting-curve substitute.
cited_facts: K491 surfaced recency-anchor gradient as emergent P3 property
K493 Phase B: linear slope -0.000216, R² 0.0103
Best fit is linear among linear/log/exponential
Score range: 0.0–0.309 across 30 keystones

insight: The highest-scoring keystones are NOT necessarily the most recent. KEYSTONE-19 ('Each Scribe a Voice. All as One.', B121, 10 days) scores 0.30890 — highest of all 30. But KEYSTONE-11 ('Help each other help ourselves.', B110, 74 days) scores 0.24574 — very high despite age. This reveals that DOMAIN DENSITY, not recency alone, drives retrieval. The Golden Key phrase is everywhere in the substrate.
rationale: Keystones touching dense vocabulary domains (cooperative, cathedral, scribe, voice) get high TF-IDF scores regardless of age. KEYSTONE-11 is the platform motto — it appears in countless reasoning moments. Its TF-IDF score rivals recent keystones because the term 'help' and 'ourselves' are densely distributed across the Eblet substrate.
cited_facts: KEYSTONE-19 score: 0.30890 (B121, 10 days old)
KEYSTONE-11 score: 0.24574 (B110, 74 days old)
KEYSTONE-5 score: 0.22788 (B110, 74 days old)
High-scoring old keystones: #5 (epistemics), #9 (planning), #11 (golden key), #14 (wells analogy)

insight: KEYSTONE-15 ('53 years of surviving the trenches of poordom, and I'm really good at it.') scores EXACTLY 0.0 — the only keystone completely invisible to TF-IDF retrieval. This is the canonical architectural gap: a keystone whose vocabulary (poordom, poverty, trenches, surviving) is entirely absent from the technical reasoning substrate.
rationale: The Eblet store contains 195 Eblets derived from K475–K491 Synapses — all technical session reasoning about platform architecture, Seer/Augur, Cathedral systems, benchmark methodology. The biographical/poverty vocabulary of KEYSTONE-15 never appears in this content. The emergent bias has completely failed for this keystone. It is effectively dead to the substrate.
cited_facts: KEYSTONE-15 top_tfidf_score: 0.00000
KEYSTONE-15 eblets_above_threshold: 0
KEYSTONE-15 direct_anchor_count: 0
Vocabulary: poordom, poverty, trenches — absent from technical Synapses

insight: The architectural mechanism producing the emergent recency gradient is NOT substrate-construction-order as hypothesized. All 195 Eblets in the store are 0-9 days old (the Eblet store was created at K485). There is no age differentiation within the Eblet store itself. The actual mechanism is: VOCABULARY COVERAGE DENSITY. Recent sessions produce Synapses about recent architectural concepts (Cathedral, Seer, Augur), and TF-IDF naturally scores queries containing those terms higher because the terms appear in many documents.
rationale: Eblet age distribution shows all 195 Eblets in the 0-9 days bucket. The K485 Eblet-construction run happened days ago, making all Eblets contemporaneous. The substrate-construction-order hypothesis cannot be validated from current data. Future analysis (K494+) with longitudinal data will be required. Instead: vocabulary density explains the pattern.
cited_facts: All 195 Eblets in 0-9 day age bucket (K485 construction was recent)
Substrate-construction-order hypothesis: cannot be validated from current store
Vocabulary coverage density: primary mechanism
High TF-IDF = high term overlap = domain is densely represented in substrate

insight: The recency-anchor gradient is best understood as a vocabulary density gradient, not a temporal decay curve. Keystones ratified in recent sessions (#17-#29) tend to cover Cathedral/Seer/Augur vocabulary that is extremely dense in the Eblet substrate (because K475-K491 sessions built those systems). Older keystones (#1-#16) are more biographical and philosophical — lower vocabulary overlap with technical substrate content.
rationale: Per-keystone analysis: B122 keystones (#20-#27) all score above 0.03, with several above 0.08. These are architectural/philosophical phrases that overlap with Cathedral vocabulary (build, voice, computer, sequence). B110 keystones split: philosophical phrases score well (#5, #9, #11) while biographical phrases score low (#3, #15). The split is THEMATIC, not purely temporal.
cited_facts: B122 keystones average score: ~0.10
B110 keystones average score: ~0.10 (similar!)
B103 keystone (#1) score: 0.06960 — moderate, not zero
KEYSTONE-15 (biographical/poverty) = 0.0 vs KEYSTONE-11 (cooperative/help) = 0.245

insight: K493 finding for K494: explicit temporal-decay TF-IDF would NOT meaningfully improve retrieval over the emergent gradient. R² = 0.0103 means age is a near-zero predictor of retrieval score. Adding explicit decay weighting (boosting recent Eblet scores) would marginally improve recency-favoring but would NOT fix KEYSTONE-15's gap (which is vocabulary, not age) and would hurt KEYSTONE-11's score (old but essential). The emergent bias is an acceptable de facto substitute for phonological keystones.
rationale: K494 should be scoped carefully: explicit decay helps for keystones whose low scores ARE caused by age (none clearly identified in this dataset — the one gap is KEYSTONE-15 which is vocabulary-driven). The better K494 intervention is VOCABULARY BRIDGING — injecting keystone vocabulary into the Eblet index explicitly for keystones below the 0.01 threshold.
cited_facts: R² = 0.0103 means age explains <2% of score variance
KEYSTONE-15 gap is vocabulary-driven, not age-driven
KEYSTONE-11 old but essential — decay weighting would harm it
K494 recommendation: vocabulary bridging over temporal decay weighting

insight: K493 Phase A established the access-frequency tracking infrastructure: last_accessed_at and access_count fields on the Eblet dataclass (with backward-compatible defaults), sidecar eblets_access_log.jsonl (append-only), EbletStore.record_access() and get_access_stats() methods, and Seer.resolve_eblet() wired to call record_access() on every pointer-resolution. The access log starts at K493 with 0 entries — future sessions will populate it, enabling genuine access-frequency analysis in K494+.
rationale: REF Staff discipline maintained: the main eblets.jsonl is append-only. Access tracking lives in a sidecar log. Existing Eblets load cleanly with access_count=0 and last_accessed_at=None defaults. New Eblets created post-K493 initialize with these fields. The instrumentation is forward-looking; retrospective data is not available.
cited_facts: Eblet schema: 9 fields → 11 fields (added last_accessed_at, access_count)
EbletStore.record_access() appends to eblets_access_log.jsonl
Seer.resolve_eblet() calls store.record_access() on successful resolution
BRIDLE check: eblets_access_log.jsonl explicitly un-ignored (.gitignore line 434)

insight: Zero of 30 Keystones have any Eblets with direct keystone_anchor match (keystone_anchors field). This is expected: KEYSTONE_PATTERNS in eblet.py only covers Keystone-28 (IP-as-filter), CJ-2298 (Seer/Augur), CJ-2287 (Synapse cluster), and a handful of Crown Jewels. The 30 numbered KEYSTONE-NN entries are NOT in KEYSTONE_PATTERNS. This means keystone_anchors is a CJ (Crown Jewel) anchor system, not a Rhetorical Keystone anchor system.
rationale: The TF-IDF retrieval proxy is therefore the PRIMARY and ONLY mechanism by which Rhetorical Keystones are retrievable from the Eblet substrate. No explicit keystone tagging exists. This is both a finding (the substrate is more dependent on TF-IDF vocabulary overlap than explicit tagging) and an architectural opportunity (adding KEYSTONE-NN detection patterns to KEYSTONE_PATTERNS would create direct anchors).
cited_facts: KEYSTONE_PATTERNS covers: Keystone-28, CJ-2298, CJ-2287, CJ-2296, CJ-cathedral, CJ-2298-virtual-memory, CJ-helm, CJ-canonical, CJ-three-fates, Keystone-provenance
KEYSTONE-00 through KEYSTONE-29: none in patterns
keystone_anchors field is populated from KEYSTONE_PATTERNS at Eblet creation time
Implication: TF-IDF is the sole retrieval path for Rhetorical Keystones

insight: The keystones with the highest 'Eblets Above Threshold' count (meaningful TF-IDF overlap with ≥ threshold score) are: KEYSTONE-25 (Basically TCP/IP, 40 Eblets), KEYSTONE-16 (tool measures value, 38 Eblets), KEYSTONE-10 (medallions minted, 35 Eblets), KEYSTONE-6 (Nothing about us, 33 Eblets). These are the best-represented keystones in the substrate. High count does not always mean high top score — it means broadly distributed coverage.
rationale: Coverage breadth (above-threshold count) differs from top-score intensity. A keystone with 40 above-threshold Eblets has broad substrate coverage. KEYSTONE-25 'TCP/IP' is broadly covered because 'protocol', 'commons', 'federation', 'transport' appear across many different Synapse clusters. Breadth is arguably more valuable than peak score for robust retrieval.
cited_facts: KEYSTONE-25: 40 Eblets above threshold (highest breadth)
KEYSTONE-16: 38 Eblets above threshold
KEYSTONE-10: 35 Eblets above threshold
KEYSTONE-1: 50 Eblets above threshold — HIGHEST (AI company tax — dense in substrate)

insight: KEYSTONE-1 ('Every AI company is currently paying a tax they don't know they're paying.', B103, 100 days old) has the HIGHEST above-threshold count: 50 Eblets. This is the oldest keystone in the dataset by session and has the broadest substrate coverage — a direct counter-example to the simple recency gradient narrative. The 'AI company tax' vocabulary (ai, company, tax, paying, alignment, model, training, cost) is ubiquitous in technical Synapses.
rationale: K491's observation that recent keystones (#28, #29) are more anchored than early ones (#1, #2) may have been query-specific. When measured systematically across all 30 keystones, KEYSTONE-1 (the oldest by B-session) has the broadest coverage. The 'recency bias' K491 observed may reflect specific query context rather than a general substrate property.
cited_facts: KEYSTONE-1: B103, 100 days old, 50 Eblets above threshold
This is the oldest keystone by ratified_session ordinal
Above-threshold count exceeds all recent B123 keystones
Challenges the simple recency-gradient narrative from K491

insight: The Founder's 'Anne Rice Vampires' hypothesis (old vampires can't evolve with the new age — old keystones naturally get forgotten) is PARTIALLY correct but the mechanism is different from expected. It's not temporal aging that causes forgetting — it's vocabulary divergence. Old keystones with vocabulary that remains relevant (KEYSTONE-1 AI company tax, KEYSTONE-11 help each other) are NOT forgotten. Only KEYSTONE-15 (biographical poverty vocabulary) is truly forgotten. The Cathedral doesn't forget uniformly — it forgets by relevance-to-current-substrate.
rationale: The Founder said 'graveyards serve a purpose for the cycle of life' and the Cathedral should naturally forget to renew. K493 empirics show the substrate is more selective: it preserves relevant old keystones while obscuring vocabulary-irrelevant ones. This is a feature — the substrate is vocabulary-adaptive, not time-adaptive. The 'old vampire' that gets stuck is one whose vocabulary was never absorbed into the technical substrate.
cited_facts: KEYSTONE-1 (oldest) = 50 above-threshold Eblets — not forgotten
KEYSTONE-15 (64 days) = 0 above-threshold Eblets — forgotten
Forgetting is vocabulary-driven, not age-driven
Anne Rice vampire = vocabulary-isolated keystone, not old keystone

insight: Three architectural recommendations emerge from K493: (1) Vocabulary Bridge Eblets — create synthetic Eblets for KEYSTONE-15 and future vocabulary-gap keystones, injecting their phrases into the substrate explicitly. (2) KEYSTONE-NN patterns in KEYSTONE_PATTERNS — add regex patterns for all 30 numbered keystones so keystone_anchors is populated correctly. (3) Defer explicit temporal-decay TF-IDF (K494/TS-001) — the emergent gradient is directionally correct but vocabulary bridging is a higher-leverage intervention than decay weighting.
rationale: K493's empirical finding changes the K494 design space. TS-001 (temporal decay TF-IDF) was scoped based on the assumption that age is the primary gradient driver. K493 shows vocabulary density is the primary driver. TS-001 would help marginally (slope is negative, decay weighting consistent with direction) but wouldn't fix the architectural gap (KEYSTONE-15) and would hurt the vocabulary-dense old keystones (KEYSTONE-11).
cited_facts: KEYSTONE-15 gap: vocabulary bridge recommended
KEYSTONE_PATTERNS extension: 30 regex patterns needed
K494 TS-001: defer or redesign as vocabulary-bridging, not temporal decay
R² = 0.0103: age has minimal predictive power for retrieval score
